summary: I was born in Thailand on June 10, 1998, after my parents fled from Burma. They entered a refugee camp in Thailand in February 1998 while my mother was pregnant with me. Life in Thailand was unstable at first because the refugee camps kept moving, but eventually, we settled in the Meh Ka Kan Camp. Thailand was the place where I spent my early childhood before moving to another camp.

Both of my parents were born in Burma, and they escaped from Burmese soldiers before coming to Thailand. My older brother was born in Burma before we fled. Burma is where my family’s difficult journey began, and it shaped much of our experience as refugees. My parents’ escape from Burma led us to live in refugee camps in Thailand.

My family finally settled in the Meh Ka Kan Camp after several moves in Thailand. The United Nations helped establish this camp and built a school for children like me. I was born in this camp, and I started nursery school there when I was five years old. We lived in Meh Ka Kan Camp for about seven years before moving to another camp.

The United Nations played an important role in our lives by helping to establish the Meh Ka Kan Camp and building a school for children. Thanks to their support, I was able to attend nursery and elementary school while living in the camp. Their assistance made life a bit more stable for families like mine during a very difficult time. The UN’s involvement was crucial for education and support in the camp.

In 2004, the Thai government relocated our family from Meh Ka Kan Camp to Mae La Oo Camp. We lived there for six years, and all my siblings went to school while we were there. Life was hard for my parents because they couldn’t find jobs and had no money, and people in the camp were not allowed to leave to work. Despite these challenges, I continued my education and wore a school uniform every day at Mae La Oo Camp.

The United States opened its doors to refugees like my family, and we applied for resettlement. In 2011, we were accepted and arrived in the U.S., specifically in St. Paul, Minnesota. Life improved a lot after we settled in the U.S., with better living conditions and new opportunities. The U.S. gave us hope and a chance to start over after many years in refugee camps.

My family and I arrived in St. Paul, Minnesota, on June 5, 2011, around midnight after being accepted for resettlement in the United States. When we arrived, we received a lot of support that helped us settle into our new life. Living in St. Paul has been much better than life in the refugee camps, and my family is happy here. This city became our new home where I started attending school and learning English.

I started attending Washington Magnet School in eighth grade in 2011 after arriving in St. Paul. At first, I didn’t know any English and had a hard time understanding my teachers and classmates. I felt lonely and wanted to go back to the refugee camp, but after a few weeks, I made friends who spoke my language. Over time, I improved my English and now I’m proud of how far I’ve come since starting at Washington Magnet School.
